subUnits.title: 6.1 Equilibrium Price and Quantity: Determinants and Applications
notes: • Definition: The price and quantity at which quantity demanded equals quantity supplied
• At equilibrium, there is no tendency for price or quantity to change
• Also called market-clearing price and quantity
• Where demand (D) and supply (S) curves intersect
• Pe = Equilibrium price, Qe = Equilibrium quantity
• Occurs when price is below equilibrium
• At P1: Qd > Qs → shortage → price tends to rise
• Occurs when price is above equilibrium
• At P2: Qs > Qd → surplus → price tends to fall
Determinants of Equilibrium Price and Quantity:
• Changes in demand or supply shift equilibrium
• Equilibrium price rises, equilibrium quantity rises
• Nigerian Example: Increased demand for rice due to population growth
→ Higher rice prices and increased rice production
• Equilibrium price falls, equilibrium quantity falls
• Equilibrium price falls, equilibrium quantity rises
• Nigerian Example: Improved farming technology increases rice supply
→ Lower rice prices and increased rice consumption
• Equilibrium price rises, equilibrium quantity falls
5. Simultaneous Changes in Demand and Supply:
• Both curves shift → effect on price and quantity depends on relative magnitude
a) Demand ↑, Supply ↑ → Quantity ↑, Price uncertain
b) Demand ↓, Supply ↓ → Quantity ↓, Price uncertain
c) Demand ↑, Supply ↓ → Price ↑, Quantity uncertain
d) Demand ↓, Supply ↑ → Price ↓, Quantity uncertain
- Set below equilibrium to make goods affordable
- Nigerian Example: Fuel price controls leading to scarcity
- Set above equilibrium to protect producers
- Nigerian Example: Minimum wage laws in labour market
• Tax on producers increases costs → supply shifts left
• Burden shared by consumers and producers
ASCII Diagram: Effect of Tax on Supply
• Nigerian Example: Import duties increase prices of imported goods
• Subsidy to producers lowers costs → supply shifts right
• Benefits shared by consumers and producers
• Nigerian Example: Fertilizer subsidies increase agricultural output
• Buffer stocks: Government buys surplus, sells during shortage
• Import quotas: Limit foreign supply to protect domestic producers
• Export bans: Keep goods domestically to stabilize prices
• Understanding equilibrium helps design price stabilization policies
• Example: Strategic grain reserves to manage food price volatility
• Exchange rate is equilibrium price of foreign currency
• Demand for forex (imports) vs supply (exports, remittances)
• Example: Naira depreciation when demand for dollars exceeds supply
• Wage is equilibrium price of labour
• Minimum wage laws create price floor
• Example: National minimum wage affecting employment levels
• Rent is equilibrium price of housing services
• Rent controls may create housing shortages
• Example: Affordable housing policies in urban areas
• 'Equilibrium means no change': Equilibrium is a tendency; markets constantly adjust
• 'Price controls always help consumers': Price ceilings create shortages; floors create surpluses
• 'Taxes always raise prices by full amount': Tax burden is shared based on elasticity
• 'Equilibrium is always desirable': Market equilibrium may not be socially optimal
Activity: Analyze equilibrium for a common good in your community (e.g., bread, fuel, mobile data)
1. Identify the good and estimate demand and supply conditions
2. Identify factors that might shift demand or supply
3. Predict how each factor would affect equilibrium price and quantity
4. Draw demand and supply curves showing predicted changes
5. Discuss: What policies could improve market outcomes?
• Equilibrium price and quantity are determined by interaction of demand and supply
• Changes in demand or supply shift equilibrium outcomes
• Understanding equilibrium helps analyze market interventions and policy impacts
• In Nigeria's dynamic economy, equilibrium analysis informs agricultural, industrial, and monetary policies
• Mastery of equilibrium concepts is essential for business strategy, policy design, and economic literacy
diagramDescriptions: Market equilibrium diagram showing intersection of demand and supply curves
Diagram of excess demand (shortage) and excess supply (surplus)
Graphs showing effects of demand and supply shifts on equilibrium
Illustration of price ceiling and price floor effects
Nigerian example: Equilibrium analysis for agricultural or forex markets
subUnits.youtubeQuery: Market equilibrium price quantity economics SSS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.explanation: Equilibrium is the price and quantity where quantity demanded equals quantity supplied, with no tendency for change.

quiz.q: If price is below equilibrium, the result is:
quiz.explanation: Price below equilibrium creates excess demand (shortage) as quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied.

quiz.q: An increase in demand with supply constant will:
quiz.explanation: Increased demand shifts the demand curve right, raising both equilibrium price and quantity.

quiz.q: An increase in supply with demand constant will:
quiz.explanation: Increased supply shifts the supply curve right, lowering equilibrium price and raising equilibrium quantity.

quiz.q: A price ceiling set below equilibrium will create:
quiz.explanation: Price ceilings below equilibrium create shortages as quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied at the controlled price.

quiz.q: A tax on producers will shift the supply curve:
quiz.explanation: Taxes increase production costs, reducing supply at every price and shifting the curve leftward.

quiz.q: If both demand and supply increase, equilibrium quantity will:
quiz.explanation: When both demand and supply increase, equilibrium quantity definitely rises, but the effect on price depends on relative magnitudes.

quiz.q: In Nigeria's foreign exchange market, naira depreciation occurs when:
quiz.explanation: When demand for foreign currency exceeds supply, the naira depreciates as its equilibrium price falls.

quiz.q: A minimum wage law is an example of:
quiz.explanation: Minimum wage sets a price floor in the labour market, potentially creating surplus labour (unemployment).

opts: Design effective market interventions and stabilization policies
quiz.explanation: Equilibrium analysis informs price controls, subsidies, taxes, and other policies to achieve desired market outcomes.
